channel.title: Research Methods course website
channel.description: Website for Spring 2026 course Research Methods

item.title: meeting dates
item.description:
Meeting Dates for the semester
We meet on alternate mondays 3pm - 4:30pm on the following dates. At the February 9th meeting we decided that most meetings will take place in person. When meeting in person, we meet in the library DMZ area in the back right classroom.
February 9
February 23

item.title: biweekly assignments
item.description:
Assignments are due every two weeks
Weeks 1-2: Foundations through Digital Worlds
Overview
Begin by mapping your current interests and questions. What draws you to media, arts, or society? Keep a research journal to track emerging curiosities.

item.title: close reading
item.description:
Close Reading and Digital Texts
Victory Garden
[Victory garden mapping in 1990s Apple software, showing the section North Garden]
Moulthrop, Stuart: Victory Garden, 1991, North Garden, disk, Eastgate Systems, Inc. (illustration: CD-ROM version for Intel Mac).

item.title: Ethnography and Observation
item.description:
Overview
Observe a media practice or cultural space—a gaming community, Instagram subculture, or streaming chat, among other examples. Take detailed field notes. What do people actually do versus what they say they do? Experiment with thick description. Consider what this method shows you that close reading couldn’t.
Methodology

item.title: Autoethnography & Critical Fabulation
item.description:
Overview
This week we look at several ‘contemporary’ techniques of scholars and researchers, including critical fabulation and autethnography.
Critical fabulation presents a practice of imagining alternative narratives or speculative scenarios based on gaps in our existing research. This is a practice of creative-critical hybrid approaches.
In autoethnography, you turn the lens on yourself, examining your own

item.title: Reading Subculture Through Zines
item.description:
Class Overview
Before diving into individual project proposals, we’ll practice close reading and ethnographic observation using physical artifacts from various subcultures: zines. Work independently to analyze unfamiliar cultural objects, then share what you discovered with the class.
Why Zines?
Zines are ephemeral, participant-created, under-documented—like many

item.title: Research Project Execution & Analysis
item.description:
Class Overview
Conduct your research. Collect data, make observations, perform your analysis, and create your documentary/podcast/multimedia piece. Stay flexible: research rarely goes exactly as planned, and adapting your approach is part of the learning. Complete your project and, importantly, reflect on your research process. What did this method reveal? What did it obscure? How did your question evolve? What would you do differently?
